era_context: Iron Age II. Shalmaneser III's reign continues in Assyria. The Levant remains under Assyrian hegemony. Israel under Ahab (or possibly his successor Ahaziah, depending on chronology) continues as a significant regional power. Phoenician city-states dominate Mediterranean trade. Egypt is fragmented. This period marks the consolidation of Assyrian power in the Near East.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Most regions outside the Assyrian sphere

events.title: Shalmaneser III's 10th regnal year campaign
events.description: Shalmaneser III's 10th regnal year campaign (c. 849 or 820 BCE depending on chronology) continues the pattern of Assyrian military expansion and consolidation in the Levant. The campaign targets Syrian and Palestinian states, reinforcing Assyrian hegemony and extracting tribute. The campaign is recorded in Assyrian annals, though the specific details and exact dating are uncertain.
events.certainty_note: The assignment of this campaign to 820 BCE is based on chronological reconstruction and is uncertain by ±5 years. Shalmaneser's regnal years are well-documented, but the absolute dating depends on which chronological system is adopted.

events.title: Reign of Ahab of Israel (end) or Ahaziah (beginning)
events.description: Depending on the chronological system used, 820 BCE may mark the end of Ahab's reign (c. 869–850 BCE in most chronologies) or the reign of his successor Ahaziah (c. 850–849 BCE). The biblical tradition records Ahab's death in battle against Aram-Damascus, though the exact date is uncertain. Ahaziah's reign is brief and marked by continued conflict with Aram and internal religious tensions. Archaeological evidence shows continued prosperity and fortification at Samaria.
key_figures: Ahab (King of Israel, possibly deceased by this year)
Ahaziah (King of Israel, possibly reigning by this year)
events.certainty_note: The regnal dates of Ahab and Ahaziah depend on chronological reconstruction. Different chronologies place Ahab's death in different years (c. 850 BCE in most systems, but ranging from c. 860–840 BCE in alternative systems). The assignment to 820 BCE is uncertain by ±10 years.

disconfirming_evidence: The chronological assignment of Ahab's death and Ahaziah's accession is highly uncertain and depends on which chronological system is adopted. The 'Low Chronology' places Ahab's death c. 850 BCE; the 'High Chronology' places it c. 860 BCE. The biblical narrative of Ahab's death in battle against Aram-Damascus is not corroborated by external sources and may be legendary. The exact year 820 BCE is not specifically documented for any event in the historical record.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 820 BCE is extremely sparse. No specific events are well-documented for this year. The only source material comes from Assyrian annals (which record military campaigns but not specific years with precision) and the Hebrew Bible (which is not contemporary and contains legendary elements). The lack of specific documentation for this year reflects the general sparsity of written records for most of the ancient world during this period. The chronological assignment of events to specific years in this era is inherently uncertain and depends on scholarly reconstruction.

graph_edges.note: Continuation of the Israelite monarchy during the Iron Age II period
